All right, welcome back in this lesson. We are going to talk about transitioning into the offer. So this is typically that big scary awkward point of a call where a lot of very kind of more sensitive or empathetic coaches get all nervous, right? Like this is where it's going to switch into the selling environment, right? Whereas they had so much fun and felt so much more at ease doing the coaching part. Now they actually got a move into selling. So the transition tends to be where a lot of coaches and enrollment specialists feel a little bit more stressed than they ought to, right? Because in reality, it should be a natural 15 to 20 second permission or consent based transition, right? And especially if you have been utterly transparent in your pre-call environment and in the agenda you said at the beginning of the call where you would explore this opportunity if things felt right, then this transition should be even easier. So how are we gonna do it? So well first we are going to really tune in and assess readiness. So once you get the sense through your presencing that the Gestalt has been unpacked as fully as it can and that you have a 51% lean towards the more positive and powered pull of those core elements, that is going to be your invitation to begin the process, right? And it's really just twofold. First ask if there's anything else they'd like to add or explore or ask or if everything feels clear so far, right? Very simple. They may bring up new questions and you may have to unpack that part of the Gestalt before reproposing through this transition. Next if they are clear, give your evaluation of them, then ask for permission. So the evaluation part is something that tends to get glossed over or missed entirely quite a bit. But you should give an evaluation here, right? Based on X, Y, or Z, based on what you achieved here, based on what you said there, based on what you're already doing or succeeding with here, I feel confident sharing more details about my program. Would you like to go through more details about what that can look like? Right? So you give a positive evaluation there, you state why you feel confident and the Y is very important there. It shows that you are actually paying attention and listening and honoring parts of what makes them empowered to go through your program. Next, you can also say or I have a bit of concern around X or Y, but based on these three things, I do feel confident extending an invitation and partnering with you. How do you feel about getting an overview of how we'd work together? So it's totally okay within your evaluation to raise a concern that comes up. And this is actually important if there is a legit concern or red flag about their ability or capacity to get optimal results. You will gain far greater trust and create far greater safety by bringing it up at this juncture. And as a general rule, you want to be able to pinpoint potential and legitimate concerns before they do. It shows A, that you're listening and B, that you do indeed have their best interest in mind and that they are safe with you, that you are living up to your declaration, that you won't allow them to make a poor decision. So, important to keep in mind, very few candidates, very few people you speak to will be an absolute 10 on 10 fifth. Most people will be in that seven to nine range. We are for the most part imperfect humans, meaning there will most likely be slight concerns that warrant being pointed out at this juncture. So don't be afraid to do so. You're not doing it to disqualify them or disqualify yourself. You're doing it to point out the legitimate and real red flag that just needs to be explored and acknowledged, not necessarily disqualify some. Later on, you can bring up that concern again when presenting the investment options and which you propose based on that concern. So, part of your concern about them is their cash flow. And you're presenting your offer with different payment options, you can recommend. Based on my concern around this, I do recommend you go with this and really pay attention to the guarantee. Track your progress over those 60 days because I really want to make sure that things are trending in the right direction. So bring up that concern again in the moment that you are proposing investment options as a way to really kind of regain their trust, right? Maintain that throughout, maintain that safety and reinforce it throughout. So by emphasizing and solving for a legitimate concern and you're solving through it based on elements of the offer that you highlight or include, whether that's payment plans, guarantees or other bonuses, but by emphasizing, calling them out and solving for it it'll be far less likely for other concerns to arise because it's only normal and natural for concerns to show up. That is what inertia is, that is what resistance is, that is what will naturally come up when someone is being called forth towards a new opportunity, right? So by solving for concerns that you point out, that you bring up that are real, true and solvable, this is what is going to allow you to play a winnable game. Solve for what is true, solve for what is real and solve for what is solvable. So draw attention to these red flags and get co-created in finding solutions to them. If they give clear, explicit and an empowered yes to moving forward into exploring the offer, then do so. However, if you feel it's more moat, more rehearsed, more compliant, you know, anything that feels a little dense or heavy once again, which you'll notice through being present, this would be the best juncture to unpack it and shift the energy. Don't take a yes wholesale. Don't even pay attention to the word yes, pay attention to how it feels because if you just pull someone along with a weak yes, chances are they're not going to really be in the right state to absorb what you have to share around your offer. So once again, this is a friendly interjection, right? I just want to invite you to take a few extra seconds right now to check in and see if you have any other questions before we move on. So don't take that yes at face value if it feels weak. Ask them to take a few more seconds to reflect on if it truly does feel good to move on. If you really feel it's a disempowered yes, if you really feel like they might not be ready, you could say I'm totally cool if you want to take some time to see how things are resonating and sitting with you and then maybe exploring options in a day or two. Would that feel better? Or are you fully up for it now? Right? So this question, this way of getting consent and permission before moving forward and making sure that it's true permission, not just well trained compliance. This is what is going to heighten and reinforce and reaffirm the safety and power they feel as you move into the next phase of your sales call. So that is a lot to consider, but this transition should be simple. It should be smooth. It should be seamless. And once again, if you are totally transparent about the fact that they are on this call, that you are on this call to explore fit and to propose an invitation. If things are fit, then this should be fun, easy and light and one of the more empowering, exciting parts of your call because it's what really shifts it to the place where they get to actualize that potential. So that's it. Take a breather and I will meet you in the next lesson. Bye for now.
